document.critique: Tested capacities, separation/same-machine rules, both exact-one constraints, and all implications. Candidate 1 survives and costs 166. Candidate 0 is feasible but costs 178; Candidate 2 violates J3/J5 separation; Candidate 3 is feasible but costs 193, not 175; Candidate 4 violates J2’s W/Z restriction. Retain the preceding proposal.

I independently checked capacities, all machine restrictions, pairwise interactions, both exactly-one rules, and conditional rules; the proposed assignment survives. Its cost is 176 (29+10+9+9+8+13+24+10+15+4+13+32). Candidate 4’s 157 is arithmetically wrong, and no tested alternative is lower, so retain the preceding proposal with corrected cost.

Retested counts, eligibility, all pairwise constraints, implications, exact-one rules, and recalculated cost. Candidate 0 survives: each machine has three jobs and total cost is 155. Candidates 1–3 are feasible but cost 164, 159, and 159, respectively. The preceding proposal is unavailable, so this exact candidate should be retained.

Rechecked all machine counts, costs, pairwise constraints, conditional rules, and both exact-one conditions. Candidate 2 fails same-machine J1/J7 and different-machine J2/J3. Candidates 0 and 1 are feasible but cost 209 and 214. Candidate 3 is feasible and costs 202, so the returned answer retains the preceding proposal.

I tested all five candidates against every speaker’s statement. None survives: Candidate 2, matching the preceding proposal, fails at least H, K, L, and O; the others fail decisive statements as well (for example A in Candidates 1, 3, and 4, and D in Candidate 0). Thus the preceding proposal cannot be retained as an exact solution.

I rechecked the proposed assignment and found G should remain a liar, but I was incorrectly marked liar: with I truthful, I's statement is true. All other statements evaluate consistently with their assigned types, including B, C, D, E, F, and G. Thus the preceding proposal must be revised only by changing I to truthful.

Re-evaluated all 17 nested statements under the preceding proposal (Candidate 1): every statement’s Boolean value matches its speaker’s assigned type. I also checked the other candidates; each has at least one mismatch (Candidate 0 already fails at F). Thus the preceding proposal survives and is the unique consistent answer.

I re-evaluated all 17 nested statements under the preceding corrected assignment. Every statement matches its speaker’s type: notably G is false because EXACTLY(2; P,O,E) is false when all three are true, and D is true. The assignment is therefore internally consistent and should be retained.

I rechecked all 14 recurrence equations under M ∈ {97,101,103}, the allowed periodic coefficients, t, r, and s. Only one system survives; its forward evaluation at n=18,19,20 gives 11, 34, and 56. The other candidates do not satisfy the inferred system, so the preceding proposal is retained.

Testing the claimed modulus-103, t=2 system against the phase-2 equations at n=6, 10, and 14 does not yield a consistent allowed coefficient set, so the preceding survival claim fails. Rechecking phase alignment indicates its proposed terms are shifted by one position. Thus Candidate 0, [42,58,17], replaces the preceding proposal.

Rechecking the indexed terms, the preceding elimination equation was incorrect: using n=4,8,12,16 gives -23p0+157q0+42t ≡ 12 (mod M). Testing all p0,q0,t ∈ {-2,-1,1,2} and M ∈ {97,101,103} yields no solution. Thus the system remains inconsistent; [0,0,0] is retained only as the stated no-solution placeholder.

I independently rechecked the n=4–17 recurrence constraints across M=97, 101, and 103, including n mod 4 coefficient selection, signed coefficient domains, r≠0, and remainder normalization. The zero continuation fails immediately; candidates 0 and 2 fail the full modular constraints. Candidate 1 remains the unique verified continuation, so I retain the preceding proposal.
